pattern: Every value's popcount-depth is a tiny integer, so maintaining one Fenwick tree per depth bucket turns range-count and point-update queries into independent prefix-sum operations.
hints: Compute each element's depth once; because a single popcount step shrinks any value drastically, only a handful of distinct depth values ever occur.
Keep one binary indexed tree per depth value, marking position j in the tree for its current depth.
A type-1 query is a range sum in the tree for depth k; a type-2 update clears the old depth's tree at idx and sets the new one.

edge_inputs.description: two elements, two range queries around an update
three elements with an update and several depth queries
update changes a depth-1 to depth-2 then back
single element queried for matching and non-matching depth
query k larger than any present depth returns zero
x=1 has depth zero, distinct from powers of two

stress.description: random initial values plus a mix of range-count and update queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size // 4)
    nums = [rng.randrange(1, 10**12) for _ in range(n)]
    q = max(1, size)
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q):
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            l = rng.randrange(0, n)
            r = rng.randrange(l, n)
            k = rng.randrange(0, 7)
            queries.append([1, l, r, k])
        else:
            idx = rng.randrange(0, n)
            val = rng.randrange(1, 10**12)
            queries.append([2, idx, val])
    return [nums, queries]
